text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Generative AI lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Generative AI produces new text, images, code, summaries, or synthetic data from prompts and examples.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Generative AI produces new text, images, code, summaries, or synthetic data from prompts and examples.
textPreview: Generative AI produces new text, images, code, summaries, or synthetic data from prompts and examples.

text: Generative AI can improve staff efficiency, but it can also produce fluent misinformation, incomplete summaries, biased language, or unsupported recommendations. Public health departments need practical rules for when generative AI can draft,.
textPreview: Generative AI can improve staff efficiency, but it can also produce fluent misinformation, incomplete summaries, biased language, or unsu...

text: Outputs can sound plausible while being wrong. Consequential outputs need subject matter review.
textPreview: Outputs can sound plausible while being wrong. Consequential outputs need subject matter review.

text:
Use this slide in the Generative AI training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 40.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Outputs can sound plausible while being wrong. Consequential outputs need subject matter review.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Generative AI in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Generative AI training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 40.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Generative AI is most useful when public health work requires drafting, summarizing, translating, organizing, or explaining information. It can reduce the time required to create first drafts, compare documents, prepare meeting materials, or convert technical findings into plain language. It should not be used as an unchecked authority on scientific evidence, law, policy, clinical guidance, or public communication.
textPreview: Generative AI is most useful when public health work requires drafting, summarizing, translating, organizing, or explaining information. ...

text: Definition: Generative AI refers to systems trained on large bodies of existing data that can produce new content, including text, images, code, summaries, and synthetic datasets, when prompted by a user.
textPreview: Definition: Generative AI refers to systems trained on large bodies of existing data that can produce new content, including text, images...

Use this slide in the Generative AI training module.
Use this slide to provide the narrative overview and definition before moving into detailed application.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 40.
Reinforce this definition: Definition: Generative AI refers to systems trained on large bodies of existing data that can produce new content, including text, images, code, summaries, and synthetic datasets, when prompted by a user.

Draft public health education materials that staff revise for accuracy, tone, literacy level, and cultural context. Summarize long guidance documents, inspection notes, surveillance updates, grant requirements, or policy changes for internal.
textPreview: Draft public health education materials that staff revise for accuracy, tone, literacy level, and cultural context. Summarize long guidan...

text: Require staff review for factual accuracy, citations, public health recommendations, legal requirements, and equity implications. Use approved source materials or retrieval-augmented generation when the output must reflect specific agency.
textPreview: Require staff review for factual accuracy, citations, public health recommendations, legal requirements, and equity implications. Use app...

text: Do not use generative AI to make final eligibility, enforcement, clinical, outbreak response, or public warning decisions. Do not rely on unsourced outputs for legal interpretation, epidemiologic conclusions, or scientific claims. Do not.

Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 40.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with High-Value Uses, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.
textPreview: Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text: Create draft messages, FAQs, or outreach language that staff can adapt for literacy level, cultural context, accessibility, and approved public health guidance.
textPreview: Create draft messages, FAQs, or outreach language that staff can adapt for literacy level, cultural context, accessibility, and approved ...

text: Summarize policy documents with human review

text:
Use this slide in the Generative AI training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 40.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

Which materials may be drafted with generative AI and which require approved templates or source documents? What human review is required before AI-assisted content is shared with the public, partners, or leadership? What data are prohibited.
textPreview: Which materials may be drafted with generative AI and which require approved templates or source documents? What human review is required...

text: Take a public health education message, a policy memo, and an internal meeting summary. Define what generative AI could help draft, what source material it would need, and what review steps would be required before use.

Use this slide in the Generative AI training module.
Use this slide for a facilitated activity near the end of the lesson.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 3, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 40.
Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Generative AI acceptable-use rules, Human review checklist for AI-assisted drafts, Approved-source guidance for RAG or document-grounded generation.
Use this topic with Plays 1, 3, 11 and Tools 2, 14, 40. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.
